description: The image content is unreadable due to insufficient resolution. Based on the provided text extraction summary, this figure likely presents experimental data for a Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W austenitic low-density steel processed with hot-rolling reductions of 45%, 68%, and 82%. The research demonstrates that increasing rolling reduction refines grain size from 21.82 μm to 7.02 μm and increases dislocation density from 2.71×10¹³ to 9.87×10¹³ m⁻², while nano-sized κ-carbides and (Nb,Mo,W)C particles remain stable. Mechanical properties show yield strength improvement from 480 MPa to 648 MPa and ultimate tensile strength from 861 MPa to 976 MPa. The figure may depict stress-strain curves, EBSD orientation maps, TEM micrographs of precipitates, or fracture surface morphologies, illustrating the strengthening mechanisms and microstructural evolution supporting enhanced mechanical properties through grain boundary and dislocation strengthening.

This figure presents comprehensive electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel subjected to varying hot-rolling reductions. Panels (a-c) display band contrast maps revealing equiaxed FCC austenite grains with numerous annealing twins in samples HR45 (45% reduction), HR68 (68% reduction), and HR82 (82% reduction), showing progressive grain refinement with increasing deformation. Panels (d-f) are phase maps overlaid with grain boundary networks, where blue represents FCC phases, black lines denote high-angle grain boundaries (θ > 15°), and red lines indicate Σ3 (60°〈111〉) twin boundaries, demonstrating decreasing twin boundary fractions (67% to 52%) but reduced effective grain sizes (21.82 μm to 7.02 μm) when twins are included. Panels (g-i) present Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps with a 0°-5° scale, revealing elevated local strain concentrations and higher geometrically necessary dislocation densities in HR82 compared to HR45. The figure collectively establishes that increasing hot-rolling reduction promotes dynamic recrystallization and grain refinement while accumulating dislocations, which contributes to enhanced strength through grain boundary and dislocation strengthening mechanisms.

This figure presents SEM backscattered-electron (BSE) images revealing the distribution of second-phase particles in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel (Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W) subjected to different hot-rolling reductions. Subfigures (a–c) at lower magnification (scale bar 20 μm) display micron-sized polygonal (Nb,Mo,W)C particles aligned along the rolling direction, while (d–f) at higher magnification (scale bar 300 nm) show submicron-sized particles homogeneously distributed within the austenite matrix. The rows correspond to samples HR45 (45% reduction), HR68 (68% reduction), and HR82 (82% reduction), respectively. The bright white contrast indicates the (Nb,Mo,W)C carbides, which are incoherent with the FCC austenite matrix and range from approximately 260 nm (submicron) to 1–25 μm (micron-sized) with a volume fraction of ~0.2%. These hard particles serve multiple roles: they act as preferential nucleation sites for particle-stimulated nucleation (PSN) during dynamic recrystallization, contributing to grain refinement with increasing rolling reduction; they provide precipitation strengthening via Orowan bypass mechanisms; however, they may also induce strain/stress concentration at interfaces, potentially serving as microcrack initiation sites during deformation.

This figure presents a comprehensive TEM characterization of a submicron-sized precipitate in the HR68 sample (Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W steel with 68% hot-rolling reduction). Panel (a) shows a TEM bright-field image revealing an approximately spherical particle with diameter ~200–300 nm. Panels (b–h) display corresponding EDS elemental mapping: Fe, Mn, and Al maps show depletion (dark contrast) within the particle, while Nb exhibits strong enrichment (bright magenta), and Mo and W show particulate distributions (orange and yellow spots), identifying the precipitate as (Nb,Mo,W)C. The carbon map (e) shows uniform background distribution. Panel (i) presents the selected-area electron diffraction pattern along the [110] zone axis, indexing to a face-centered cubic (FCC) crystal structure with labeled reflections (002), (111), and (1̄11), confirming the MC-type carbide structure with lattice parameter 4.36 Å. These submicron particles are homogeneously distributed in the austenite matrix and contribute to strengthening via Orowan bypass mechanisms.

This figure presents transmission electron microscopy characterization of κ-carbide precipitates in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel subjected to varying hot-rolling reductions. Panels (a–c) show selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns along the [1 0 0] zone axis for samples HR45 (45% reduction), HR68 (68% reduction), and HR82 (82% reduction), respectively. The faint (0 0 1) and (0 1 0) superlattice reflections marked with circles indicate the presence of ordered κ-carbides with an L′1₂-type structure formed via spinodal decomposition during water quenching. Panels (d–f) display corresponding dark-field (DF) images captured using the (1 0 0) superlattice reflection, revealing homogeneously distributed nano-sized precipitates within the austenite matrix. The caption reports mean κ-carbide sizes of 0.81±0.26 nm, 0.78±0.17 nm, and 0.83±0.23 nm, and volume fractions of 3.5±0.33%, 3.3±0.52%, and 3.9±0.39% for HR45, HR68, and HR82 respectively. These results demonstrate that increasing hot-rolling reduction from 45% to 82% does not significantly alter the size or volume fraction of κ-carbides, confirming their contribution to precipitate strengthening alongside dislocation and grain boundary strengthening mechanisms.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W austenitic low-density steel subjected to different hot-rolling reductions (45%, 68%, and 82%), labeled as HR45, HR68, and HR82 respectively. Subfigure (a) displays room-temperature engineering stress-strain curves, where the blue curve (HR82) exhibits the highest ultimate tensile strength (~976 MPa) and yield strength (~648 MPa), while the gray curve (HR45) shows the lowest strength but highest ductility (~60% total elongation). Subfigure (b) illustrates Charpy V-notch impact energy decreasing linearly from 133±2.5 J/m² at 45% reduction to 105±1.2 J/m² at 82% reduction. The data demonstrate that increasing hot-rolling reduction enhances both yield strength (~170 MPa improvement) and ultimate tensile strength (~110 MPa improvement) through grain refinement and dislocation strengthening, with only minor reductions in uniform elongation (52% to 39%) and total elongation (60% to 50%). Despite the strength-ductility trade-off, all samples maintain excellent toughness above 100 J/m² and reduction of area exceeding 60%, indicating that the microstructural optimization via thermomechanical processing achieves superior mechanical property balance.

This figure presents SEM fractography of tensile-fractured Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples subjected to different hot-rolling reductions: (a) HR45 (45% reduction), (b) HR68 (68% reduction), and (c) HR82 (82% reduction). All micrographs, captured at a scale of 20 μm, reveal typical ductile fracture morphology characterized by numerous dimples (circled) and microvoids (indicated by arrows). The fracture surfaces exhibit a mixture of fine equiaxed dimples and larger microvoids nucleated at (Nb,Mo,W)C particles. Notably, the dimple size in HR45 is visibly larger than in HR68 and HR82, correlating with its larger mean grain size (21.82 μm vs. 11.55 μm and 7.02 μm). The microvoids form via debonding of intragranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles, alleviating strain concentration at intergranular carbide interfaces. Despite increasing rolling reduction from 45% to 82%, the ductile fracture characteristic is retained with abundant dimples observed across all samples, indicating that toughness remains largely unaffected. This demonstrates that the intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles, while acting as preferential nucleation sites for microcracks, do not detrimentally impact toughness due to stress relief through microvoid formation.

This dual-Y-axis scatter plot illustrates the trade-off between yield strength (Rp0.2, MPa) and two critical mechanical properties: Charpy impact energy (J/cm², left blue axis) and total elongation (%, right red axis) for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steels and benchmark alloys. The X-axis spans 300–1000 MPa yield strength. Solid blue symbols (circles, triangles, squares) represent the investigated Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W steel processed with different hot-rolling reductions (45%, 68%, 82%), showing a gradual increase in yield strength from ~480 MPa to ~648 MPa with decreasing Charpy energy from ~133 J/cm² to ~105 J/cm² while maintaining high total elongation of 50–60%. Green stars mark specific high-performance states of the studied alloy. Red open symbols denote comparison materials including solution-treated austenitic stainless steels and other Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys, which typically exhibit lower elongation (<40%) and impact toughness at similar or lower strength levels. The data demonstrate that increasing hot-rolling reduction refines grain size and increases dislocation density, achieving significant strengthening without sacrificing the excellent ductility and toughness characteristic of high-manganese austenitic steels, outperforming conventional age-hardened steels which show comparable strength but markedly inferior ductility (~21%) and toughness.

Figure 8 presents quantitative microstructural evolution data for Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W austenitic low-density steel subjected to varying hot-rolling reductions (45%, 68%, and 82%). Panel (a) illustrates that increasing hot-rolling reduction progressively refines the mean grain size from approximately 45 μm to 19 μm (excluding twins) and from 22 μm to 7 μm (effective size including twin boundaries), while the geometrically necessary dislocation density increases monotonically from 2.7×10^13 m^-2 to 9.9×10^13 m^-2. Panel (b) demonstrates that both the volume fractions (~3.5% for κ-carbides and ~0.2% for (Nb,Mo,W)C) and mean particle sizes (~0.8 nm for κ-carbides and ~260 nm for (Nb,Mo,W)C) remain essentially constant regardless of rolling reduction. These results indicate that the observed strength enhancement with increased rolling reduction primarily originates from grain boundary strengthening (including twin boundary contributions) and dislocation strengthening, whereas precipitate strengthening remains unchanged due to the stable carbide characteristics.

Figure 9 presents quantitative strengthening mechanism analysis for a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel subjected to different hot-rolling reductions (HR45, HR68, and HR82 representing 45%, 68%, and 82% reduction, respectively). Subfigure (a) displays the Hall-Petch relationship plotting the true flow stress at yielding σF0 (obtained by subtracting precipitate strengthening σp and dislocation strengthening σds from yield strength) as a function of the inverse square root of mean effective grain size de^(-1/2). The linear fitting yields a friction stress σ0 of 203.1 MPa and Hall-Petch coefficient of 733.4 MPa·μm^(1/2), with data points showing σF0 increasing from 359.5 MPa (21.82 μm) to 479.3 MPa (7.02 μm) as grain size refines. Subfigure (b) shows stacked bar charts illustrating calculated contributions of individual strengthening mechanisms to yield strength: friction stress (σF, cross-hatched), precipitate shearing (σp-shearing, light red), Orowan bypass (σp-Orowan, red), dislocation strengthening (σds, light blue), and grain boundary strengthening (σgb, dark blue). Grain boundary strengthening (including high-angle grain boundaries and annealing twin boundaries) dominates and increases significantly from HR45 to HR82, while dislocation strengthening also contributes substantially. White triangles indicate experimentally measured yield strengths (σexp), showing good agreement with calculated values and validating that grain boundary and dislocation strengthening are primarily responsible for the strength increment with increasing hot-rolling reduction.

The figure displays true stress (σt)-strain (εt) curves (solid lines) superimposed with strain-hardening rate (dσ/dε)-true strain curves (dotted lines) for a Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C austenitic low-density steel processed at different hot-rolling reductions: HR45 (45%), HR68 (68%), and HR82 (82%). The graph reveals a characteristic three-staged strain-hardening behavior across all samples: an initial sharp drop after yielding (Stage I), followed by a gradual increase to peak values (Stage II), and subsequent continuous decrease until fracture (Stage III). Arrows indicate interrupted tensile test strains at εc ≈ 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4. With increasing hot-rolling reduction, strain-hardening rates progressively increase, maintaining a high level of approximately 2000 MPa over a wide plastic strain range, which contributes to the excellent ductility. This three-staged behavior, distinct from conventional high-stacking-fault-energy (SFE ~73 mJ/m²) FCC metals that show monotonically decreasing rates, supports the mechanism for improved strength-ductility combinations in these steels.

This figure presents nine TEM bright-field (BF) images captured along the 〈1 1 0〉 zone axis of the austenite matrix, illustrating the evolution of deformation substructures in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples subjected to different hot-rolling reductions: HR45 (45%, a-c), HR68 (68%, d-f), and HR82 (82%, g-i). The micrographs document the microstructural development at engineering strains (εe) of 0.1 (a, d, g), 0.2 (b, e, h), and 0.4 (c, f, i), with scale bars of 500 nm (a-b, d-e, g-h) and 1 μm (c, f, i). At low strain (0.1), homogeneous arrays of planar slip bands and Taylor lattices formed by intersecting non-coplanar {1 1 1} slip traces (dashed lines) are visible across all samples. As strain increases to 0.2, slip bands become more closely spaced, with microbands beginning to form in HR68 and HR82 (e, h). At high strain (0.4), well-developed microbands with distinct boundaries (solid lines) and ill-defined boundaries (arrows) dominate the microstructure. The figure demonstrates that higher rolling reductions (finer initial grain sizes) accelerate deformation substructure evolution, evidenced by reduced slip band spacing and earlier microband formation, supporting the three-staged strain-hardening mechanism involving slip band refinement followed by microband accommodation.

The figure presents the evolution of slip band spacing as a function of engineering strain for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel subjected to different hot-rolling reductions (HR45, HR68, and HR82 representing 45%, 68%, and 82% reduction, respectively). The plot displays three distinct curves with error bars: HR45 (gray squares) starts with the largest spacing (296.40 ± 50.06 nm at strain 0.1) and decreases gradually to approximately 55 nm at strain 0.4; HR68 (red circles) begins at 155.02 ± 38.42 nm and stabilizes around 50-55 nm after strain 0.2; HR82 (blue triangles) shows the smallest initial spacing (68.05 ± 8.91 nm) and quickly saturates at approximately 50 nm. All samples exhibit decreasing slip band spacing with increasing plastic strain, eventually saturating at approximately 50 nm. The data demonstrate that higher hot-rolling reductions (finer initial grain sizes) result in faster slip band refinement kinetics and earlier saturation, attributed to higher accumulated back stress on dislocation sources in smaller grains. This accelerated evolution leads to earlier microband formation, explaining the reduced duration of stage II strain hardening and slight ductility loss in heavily rolled samples, while contributing to enhanced strain-hardening rates through increased long-range stress between slip bands.
